did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a multi-scale AI deep learning CNN technique for skin cancer detection. Specifically, it employs transfer learning with pre-trained weights from the ImageNet dataset to build a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for classifying melanoma versus benign tumors. The method involves image preprocessing, data augmentation, and combining outputs from three separately trained CNNs into a Fully Interconnected Network (FCN). The study achieved an accuracy of 90.09% on a test dataset derived from the ISIC 2020 sample.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and validation of a multi-scale AI deep learning CNN technique for skin cancer detection. It details specific methodology (transfer learning, data augmentation, CNN architecture), describes the dataset used (ISIC 2020), and reports original results (90.09% accuracy). Although it includes a literature review section, its primary purpose is to report new technical findings and performance metrics, classifying it as a research paper (specifically a conference paper).

title: Skin Cancer Detection Using A Multi-Scale AI Deep Learning CNN Techniques
journal: 2025 6th International Conference on Recent Advances in Information Technology (RAIT)
brief_summary: This study proposes a multi-scale AI deep learning CNN technique for detecting melanoma versus benign skin tumors. The methodology employs transfer learning with ImageNet pre-trained weights, image preprocessing, and data augmentation to address class imbalance in the ISIC 2020 dataset. Specifically, the approach combines outputs from three separately trained CNNs into a Fully Interconnected Network (FCN) to focus on lesion regions. The model was evaluated on a test set comprising 4% of the ISIC 2020 sample. The proposed method achieved an accuracy of 90.09%, outperforming several state-of-the-art approaches in terms of recall and precision.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target class for detection. The study focuses on differentiating melanoma from benign tumors/non-melanoma lesions using the ISIC 2020 dataset.

The study performs binary classification (melanoma vs. non-melanoma). The dataset (ISIC 2020) contains nine skin condition categories, which were balanced to address class imbalance, but specific labels for these benign classes are not enumerated in the text.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into melanoma versus non-melanoma (benign tumors) categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the model was modified for 'binary classification, concentrating on melanoma and non-melanoma.' It replaces the final softmax layer for ImageNet categories with one for this binary task. The study evaluates performance using accuracy (90.09%), recall, and precision on the ISIC 2020 dataset.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2020
datasets_used.description: A sample from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions. The study utilized 33,126 images from 2,000 people.

use.training: Used for training the multi-scale AI deep learning CNN technique after balancing the class distribution and applying data augmentation (creating 35 variants by rotating images by 10 degrees).
use.testing: Used for final evaluation, comprising approximately 4% (1,400 photos) of the total sample.

datasets_used.name: ImageNet
datasets_used.description: A large-scale hierarchical image database used for pre-training weights in the transfer learning approach.

use.training: Used to initialize the network's weights via transfer learning before modifying the final softmax layer for the binary classification task.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used to evaluate classification tasks (melanoma vs. all non-melanoma lesions and melanoma vs. atypical lesions) in a cited study by Esteva et al. [5] for comparison purposes.
Used to ensure the model correctly prioritized minority classes by assigning unique categorization costs to each class.
Used to address class imbalance in the dataset by raising the representation of minority classes or decreasing the number of majority classes.
Used to visualize and analyze the performance of the proposed approach, including accuracy and loss metrics.
Used to evaluate the diagnostic capacity and performance of the model, particularly regarding the prioritization of minority classes.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, recall, precision, sensitivity, specificity, ROC curves, and AUC, with comparisons made to other methods based on these metrics rather than statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2020.notes: The proposed method outperformed state-of-the-art approaches in recall and precision, while performing equally in accuracy. The study utilized 33,126 images from the ISIC 2020 sample, with 1,400 images (4%) reserved for testing. The training set consisted of 31,726 photos with data augmentation (35 variants by rotating images by 10 degrees). The model achieved a training accuracy between 70% and 75% after 25 epochs. The accuracy disparity between validation and training sets was reduced from 20% to 2-3% using dropout and augmentation.

ISIC 2016.notes: Results from Ali et al. are cited in the Related Works section for comparison purposes.

ISIC Archive (Esteva et al. [5]).notes: Results from Esteva et al. are cited in the Related Works section. The dataset contained 2,624 melanomas, 144 uncommon nevi, and 2,146 benign lesions. Evaluation was performed using two-fold cross-validation twenty times.

Dorge et al. Dataset [2].notes: Results from Dorge et al. are cited in the Related Works section. The study tested their method on 3,753 photos of four different kinds of skin cancer.

Rashid et al. Dataset [10].notes: Results from Rashid et al. are cited in the Related Works section. They employed GANs on the ISIC 2018 dataset.

Bisla et al. Dataset [11].notes: Results from Bisla et al. are cited in the Related Works section. They enhanced ISIC 2017 and ISIC 2018 datasets using deep adaptive GANs.

Ali et al. Dataset [12].notes: Results from Ali et al. are cited in the Related Works section. They used a self-focusing PGAN on the ISIC 2018 dataset.

architecture_used.main_model: Multi-scale AI Deep Learning CNN (specifically, a combination of three separately trained Convolutional Neural Networks combined into a Fully Interconnected Network (FCN))
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning with pre-trained weights from the ImageNet dataset (1,000 categories)
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported (images normalized by averaging RGB values)

architecture_details.backbone: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) initialized with ImageNet weights
architecture_details.structure: Three separate CNNs trained on different stages focusing on the image center (suspected lesion), with outputs combined into a Fully Interconnected Network (FCN)
architecture_details.heads: Binary classification head (replacing the final softmax layer of the pre-trained model)
architecture_details.classifier_head: Binary classification layer for melanoma vs. non-melanoma (benign tumors)
architecture_details.activation: Sigmoid function mentioned as an alternative for binary classification

training_details.loss_function: Not explicitly reported (loss curves shown in Fig. 5)

hyperparameters.learning_rate: Moderate learning rate (exact value not reported)
hyperparameters.epochs: 25 epochs reported for initial training phase
hyperparameters.dropout: Dropout layers used to reduce overfitting

training_details.training_schedule: Initial training on original dataset without augmentation, followed by training on balanced dataset with data augmentation (35 variants by rotating images by 10 degrees)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Multi-scale AI Deep Learning CNN (Proposed Method)
Two-level approach incorporating adversarial training with transfer learning
preprocessing_techniques: Normalization by averaging each image's green, red, and blue (RGB) values
Data augmentation via rotation (creating 35 variants by rotating images by 10 degrees)
Dataset balancing to address class imbalance among the nine skin condition categories

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Images were rotated by 10 degrees to create 35 variants for each image, resulting in a training set of 31,726 photos.
Resampling strategies were used to address class imbalance among the nine skin condition categories, raising the representation of minority classes or decreasing the number of majority classes.
Used to prioritize minority classes by assigning unique categorization costs to each class.
Mentioned as a method used to improve the model's capacity to recognize characteristics of underrepresented groups.

conclusions: The proposed multi-scale AI deep learning CNN technique, which combines outputs from three separately trained CNNs into a Fully Interconnected Network (FCN), achieved an accuracy of 90.09% on the ISIC 2020 test dataset.
The method outperformed state-of-the-art approaches in terms of recall and precision, while performing equally in accuracy compared to other methods.
The approach improves detection accuracy by focusing on the center of the image where the suspected lesion is located, thereby maximizing feature extraction from the questionable skin area.
Dataset balancing and data augmentation (creating 35 variants by rotating images by 10 degrees) were crucial for addressing class imbalance and improving model performance.
The accuracy disparity between validation and training sets was reduced from 20% to 2-3% through the use of dropout layers and data augmentation, effectively mitigating overfitting.

The model's training accuracy remained between 70% and 75% after 25 epochs, indicating that the model may not have fully learned all pertinent characteristics, potentially due to initial data imbalance.
Cost-sensitive learning was employed to ensure the model correctly prioritized minority classes by assigning unique categorization costs to each class.

The study demonstrates that combining feature extraction and classification at the same time in deep learning, specifically by utilizing information found in the center areas of skin cancer photos, enhances diagnostic capacity.
important_remarks: The study is a conference paper (RAIT 2025) with limited scope and peer-review depth compared to journal publications.

The dataset (ISIC 2020) was heavily augmented (35 variants per image via 10-degree rotation) to create a training set of 31,726 images from 33,126 total images; this synthetic augmentation may not fully represent real-world clinical variability.
The model achieved a training accuracy of only 70-75% after 25 epochs, suggesting the model may not have fully converged or learned all pertinent features, potentially due to initial data imbalance.

The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the comparison against state-of-the-art methods; performance claims are based on point estimates of accuracy, recall, and precision.

External validation is lacking; the model was evaluated solely on a test set derived from the ISIC 2020 dataset, with no mention of validation on independent, multi-center, or diverse demographic datasets.

The study focuses on binary classification (melanoma vs. non-melanoma) using a subset of the ISIC 2020 data, ignoring the multi-class nature of the full dataset, which limits clinical applicability for differential diagnosis.

The hardware specifications (Intel Core i5, 3200 MHz DDR4 RAM) are modest for deep learning, raising questions about the computational efficiency and scalability of the proposed multi-scale CNN architecture.

The paper acknowledges that the accuracy disparity between validation and training sets was reduced from 20% to 2-3% using dropout and augmentation, but notes that the model's accuracy 'significantly declined and gotten worse' in some contexts compared to vanilla models, indicating potential instability.
Cost-sensitive learning was used to prioritize minority classes, but the specific costs assigned are not detailed, making reproducibility difficult.

The comparison with state-of-the-art methods (e.g., Harangi et al., Kalouche et al.) is indirect, as the paper notes that 'various approaches could not employ the same test collections,' limiting the validity of direct performance comparisons.
